name: New Brunswick
history: New Brunswick was one of the original four provinces that formed the Dominion of Canada in 1867, though European settlement had begun much earlier with French Acadians in the 17th century. The region played a crucial role in the American Revolution as a loyalist stronghold, with thousands of United Empire Loyalists fleeing the newly independent United States and establishing communities throughout the province. The economy historically relied on timber harvesting, shipbuilding, and fishing, industries that shaped the character and settlement patterns of coastal and inland communities. Today, New Brunswick retains strong connections to its maritime heritage and French colonial past.
culturalNotes: New Brunswick is Canada's only officially bilingual province, with significant francophone and anglophone communities that have maintained distinct cultural traditions for centuries. The province is known for its strong Acadian heritage, particularly in the southeastern regions, celebrated through festivals, cuisine featuring seafood and traditional recipes, and the iconic Acadian flag. The Bay of Fundy, which borders the province, is central to regional identity, offering the world's highest tides and inspiring local art, literature, and outdoor traditions.
bestTimeToVisit: Visit in summer (June-September) for beach activities and the Bay of Fundy tides.
languageNotes: English and French are both official languages, with French particularly prevalent in the Acadian regions of southeastern New Brunswick. Common local phrases include 'Bonjour' and 'Hello' as dual greetings, reflecting the bilingual nature of daily life in many communities.

places.name: Saint John
places.description: New Brunswick's largest city, Saint John is a historic port with stunning Victorian architecture and a vibrant waterfront district. The city serves as a gateway to the Bay of Fundy and offers excellent museums, restaurants, and cultural attractions.

activities.detail: Experience the world's highest tides by taking a guided boat tour from the Saint John waterfront to witness the dramatic tidal bore and marine wildlife.
Browse Canada's oldest continuously operating farmer's market (since 1876) housed in a historic building featuring local produce, crafts, and prepared foods.
Explore Canadian and international art collections in a restored Victorian mansion showcasing contemporary and historical works.
Witness the unique natural phenomenon where the Bay of Fundy's tides reverse the flow of the Saint John River twice daily.
Step back in time at this restored 1880s general store that authentically recreates maritime shopping and community life.
Hike scenic forest and coastal trails along the Bay of Fundy shoreline just outside the city with opportunities to spot seabirds and marine life.
Discover the history and heritage of Saint John's Jewish community in Canada's oldest Jewish congregation museum.

food.detail: A local favorite featuring fresh Bay of Fundy lobster served on a toasted bun with minimal mayo, highlighting the sweet maritime flavors.
Crispy roasted seaweed harvested from the Bay of Fundy, often salted and sold as a regional specialty throughout Saint John.
A traditional Maritime dish of grated potatoes mixed with chicken or seafood and baked in a savory broth, reflecting Acadian heritage.
A creamy soup featuring Bay of Fundy scallops, a signature dish found in waterfront restaurants celebrating local seafood.
A Halifax-style meat sandwich with sweet donair sauce that has become increasingly popular in Saint John's diverse food scene.

culture.detail: Plan activities around the Bay of Fundy's tides, which fluctuate dramatically and repeat on a roughly 12-hour cycle, affecting waterfront access and tours.
Saint John's identity is deeply rooted in its role as a historic port and fishing hub, so respect and interest in nautical history and shipbuilding traditions is appreciated.
Visitors may notice distinctive Maritime English pronunciation and expressions unique to Atlantic Canada that add character to conversations with locals.
The revitalized waterfront district is best explored on foot; many attractions, restaurants, and galleries cluster near the harbor within easy walking distance.

places.name: Fredericton
places.description: The provincial capital, Fredericton is a charming river city known for its tree-lined streets, literary heritage, and cultural institutions. The city features beautiful riverside parks, historic sites, and a thriving arts and dining scene.

activities.detail: Stroll along the scenic Riverfront Trail for 10 kilometers past tree-lined pathways, viewing platforms, and public art installations overlooking the Saint John River.
Tour this stunning Gothic Revival cathedral completed in 1853, featuring intricate stained glass windows and notable wood carvings.
Explore this world-class art museum housed in a historic building, showcasing works by Canadian and international artists including paintings by Salvador Dalí and the Group of Seven.
Discover Fredericton's local history through exhibits on indigenous heritage, colonial settlement, and the famous Axeman of Oz legend.
Visit this picturesque parade square surrounded by restored historic buildings, hosting the daily Changing of the Guard ceremony during summer months.
Explore 160 hectares of mixed forest with hiking trails, wildlife observation opportunities, and botanical gardens just outside the city center.

food.detail: A traditional Maritime dish of layered grated potatoes, meat broth, and poultry, found at local restaurants celebrating regional Acadian cuisine.
Locally harvested Atlantic seaweed preparations used in salads, snacks, and specialty foods reflecting New Brunswick's coastal culinary traditions.
Fresh Atlantic salmon from the Saint John River, prepared grilled or smoked at upscale dining establishments throughout the city.
A hearty Acadian stew made with chicken, potatoes, and onions served in a savory broth, available at family-style restaurants.
Locally sourced wild blueberries featured in pies, jams, and baked goods at cafés and markets reflecting New Brunswick's berry heritage.

culture.detail: Fredericton is deeply connected to Canadian literature through writers like Lord Byron and Bliss Carman; explore the literary walking tours and bookstores that celebrate this tradition.
The city is known as Canada's City of Trees, so visitors should respect the verdant landscape and enjoy the many tree-lined streets as a defining feature of local identity.
The Saint John River is central to Fredericton's identity and recreation culture; locals take pride in riverside activities and seasonal water traditions.
Fredericton has a vibrant grassroots arts community with frequent gallery openings, street festivals, and live performances, so check local event calendars for unexpected cultural experiences.

places.name: Hopewell Rocks
places.description: A stunning natural wonder featuring towering rock formations that are dramatically revealed and concealed by the Bay of Fundy's extreme tides. Visitors can kayak around the rocks at high tide and walk on the ocean floor at low tide, making it New Brunswick's most iconic natural attraction.

activities.detail: Paddle around the base of the towering rock formations when water levels are at their peak, offering a unique perspective of the 40-meter cliffs from sea level.

activities.text: Walking the Ocean Floor at Low Tide
activities.detail: Explore the exposed ocean floor between the rocks during low tide, when the Bay of Fundy recedes up to 12 meters and reveals a landscape usually hidden beneath water.
Walk the scenic coastal trail that winds through the park offering multiple viewpoints of the distinctive flowerpot-shaped rock formations and surrounding bay vistas.
Learn about the Bay of Fundy's extraordinary tidal phenomenon, geology, and marine ecosystems through interactive exhibits and educational displays at the visitor center.
Descend the cliff faces or climb guided rock routes on the formations for adventure seekers looking for a more thrilling experience of the landscape.
Capture the dramatic transformation of the rocks throughout the day as tides shift, creating ever-changing lighting and perspectives for landscape photography.

food.detail: Sample locally-caught lobster prepared simply steamed or in lobster rolls at waterfront restaurants, showcasing the pristine seafood harvested from these famous tidal waters.
Try the red seaweed (dulse) harvested from the Bay of Fundy, often sold dried and seasoned as a crunchy local snack with roots in Maritime food traditions.
Enjoy hearty bowls of New Brunswick seafood chowder made with local fish, scallops, and mussels from the tidal waters surrounding the rocks.
Taste this traditional Acadian dish of grated potato and chicken in a savory sauce, reflecting the French heritage prevalent throughout the Fundy region.
Order battered and fried local whitefish or haddock served with hand-cut fries at casual waterfront eateries near the park entrance.

culture.detail: Check tide tables carefully before visiting as the 12-meter tidal swing defines the entire experience, with low tide occurring at different times each day due to lunar cycles.
When walking the exposed ocean floor at low tide, wear sturdy water shoes or boots as the seabed is rocky, slippery, and covered with seaweed and barnacles.
The surrounding Moncton and Hopewell area has a strong Acadian cultural presence; acknowledge this through visiting Acadian museums and supporting local French-language businesses.
The rising tide moves rapidly across the bay floor, so always return well before the predicted high tide time to avoid being cut off by incoming water.

places.name: Acadian Coast
places.description: Stretching along the eastern shore, the Acadian Coast celebrates the rich French-Acadian culture with charming fishing villages, seafood restaurants, and warm hospitality. The region offers pristine beaches, fresh local cuisine, and authentic cultural experiences.

activities.detail: Paddle around the iconic rock formations at high and low tide to experience the Bay of Fundy's dramatic 40-foot tidal range.
Explore a recreated 19th-century Acadian settlement with costumed interpreters, heritage buildings, and traditional crafts near Moncton.
Wade into the warmest saltwater beach in Canada at Shediac, known as the scallop capital with sandy shores and family amenities.
Hike coastal trails through boreal forest and salt marshes while spotting seals, beavers, and migratory birds along the Gulf of Saint Lawrence.
Attend seasonal festivals celebrating local catches with live music, cooking demonstrations, and endless fresh seafood tastings.
Learn about Acadian resilience and culture at this museum dedicated to the deportation, survival, and revival of the Acadian people.

food.detail: A hearty traditional Acadian dish made with grated potatoes, meat broth, and chicken or seafood baked in a rich, savory crust.
Sweet, tender Atlantic lobster sourced directly from local waters and served boiled, grilled, or in traditional Acadian preparations.
A comforting potato and seafood stew seasoned with salt pork and served in a creamy broth, reflecting coastal heritage cooking.
Shediac's signature sweet scallops, grilled or pan-seared and best enjoyed at waterfront restaurants overlooking the harbor.
Grated potato dumplings with a pork center, boiled and served as a traditional Acadian comfort food side dish or light meal.
Creamy New England-style soup loaded with local haddock, mussels, and clams, a staple in village restaurants throughout the coast.

culture.detail: Locals appreciate visitors attempting Chiac, a unique Acadian dialect blending French and English; simple greetings go a long way.
The Acadian people endured deportation and cultural suppression; showing genuine interest in their heritage and museums is deeply valued.
Many cultural events and seafood celebrations peak June through August; timing your visit around local festivals enriches the authentic experience.
Dining at independent establishments run by fishing families directly supports the community and guarantees the freshest, most authentic meals.

places.name: Moncton
places.description: The largest city in Atlantic Canada by population, Moncton is a vibrant hub for shopping, dining, and entertainment with excellent modern facilities. The city is centrally located for exploring the Maritime region and features attractions like the Bore and diverse cultural offerings.

activities.detail: Witness the dramatic tidal bore phenomenon on the Shubenacadie River, where ocean tides create a visible wave that surges upstream, a unique natural spectacle best experienced during high tides.
Drive up this optical illusion hill where gravity appears to reverse and objects seem to roll uphill, a quirky roadside attraction that has puzzled visitors for decades.
Browse this historic public market featuring local vendors selling fresh produce, seafood, artisanal goods, and prepared foods in a vibrant downtown setting.
Explore this interactive cultural centre showcasing Moncton's renaissance story through exhibits on local history, innovation, and the city's transformation.
Visit the Bay of Fundy's dramatic tidal formations about 45 minutes from Moncton, where you can kayak at high tide and walk on the ocean floor at low tide.
Enjoy family-friendly indoor entertainment featuring arcade games, mini golf, laser tag, and bumper cars in this covered entertainment complex.

food.detail: A traditional Maritime comfort dish of grated potato mixed with chicken or seafood, spices, and broth, baked until golden and crispy on top.
Atlantic lobster meat tossed with minimal mayo and served on a toasted roll, highlighting the region's premier seafood catch.
Moncton's take on the Halifax favourite—spiced meat in a pita with sweet donair sauce—reflecting the city's multicultural food scene.
Creamy bisque loaded with local cod, shrimp, mussels, and Maritime vegetables, served in many local restaurants and markets.
New Brunswick wild blueberries feature prominently in local desserts, pies, and jams available at markets and specialty shops.

culture.detail: Moncton's identity is deeply tied to the Bay of Fundy's dramatic tides; plan activities around tide schedules and ask locals for insider timing tips.
New Brunswick is officially bilingual (English and French), and Moncton reflects this heritage; French is widely spoken and many signs are bilingual.
Check for seasonal events like the Fringe Festival, Jazz Festival, and Acadian Festival, which showcase local culture and draw visitors year-round.
Monctonians take pride in supporting local businesses and independent retailers; neighbourhood shops and markets are preferred over big-box stores.

places.name: Fundy National Park
places.description: A spectacular wilderness park featuring dramatic coastal cliffs, waterfalls, and hiking trails that showcase the natural beauty of the Bay of Fundy region. The park offers camping, scenic drives, and access to pristine beaches and forest ecosystems.

activities.detail: A popular 2.5 km loop trail leading to a scenic 40-meter waterfall surrounded by old-growth forest.
Visit the iconic flowerpot-shaped rock formations that are accessible on foot at low tide and by boat at high tide, showcasing the Bay of Fundy's extreme 12-meter tidal range.
Adventure into sea caves accessible during specific tidal windows to witness coastal geology and marine ecosystems up close.

activities.text: Scenic Drive on Fundy Park Loop
activities.detail: Travel the park's main road offering multiple viewpoints of coastal cliffs, valleys, and the Bay of Fundy landscape.
Stay overnight in the heart of the park with access to amenities and trailheads for early morning hikes.
Discover marine life in exposed tidal pools at low tide, including starfish, sea anemones, and crustaceans.

food.detail: Sample locally-caught Atlantic lobster prepared simply at park restaurants and nearby coastal establishments to highlight its natural sweetness.
Try this Maritime specialty—a spiced ground meat sandwich served with sweet donair sauce—a beloved regional favorite throughout New Brunswick.
Experience this traditional French-Acadian dish made with grated potato and meat, reflecting the cultural heritage of the Bay of Fundy region.
Enjoy local wild blueberry pie, jam, or fresh berries sourced from the forests surrounding Fundy National Park.
Purchase locally-smoked salmon from regional producers that showcase the cold waters of the Bay of Fundy.

culture.detail: Plan all beach and cave activities around published tide tables, as the Bay of Fundy's extreme tides create rapidly changing conditions that are essential for safe exploration.
The park sits on traditional Mi'kmaq territory; respect cultural sites and consider interpretive programs that explain Indigenous connections to this landscape.
Coastal conditions change rapidly with fog, wind, and sudden temperature drops common even in summer, so bring layered clothing regardless of the forecast.
The park's fragile ecosystems require visitors to stay on marked trails, pack out all waste, and avoid disturbing wildlife and plants in this protected wilderness.

places.name: Magnetic Hill
places.description: A quirky natural optical illusion near Moncton where gravity appears to work backwards, causing vehicles to roll uphill on their own. This beloved roadside attraction has charmed visitors for decades and remains a popular photo opportunity.

activities.detail: Put your vehicle in neutral at the designated spot and feel the mysterious force that appears to pull your car uphill against gravity.
Explore this family-friendly zoo located adjacent to the hill featuring over 400 animals from around the world.
Capture memorable shots at the iconic Magnetic Hill sign and marker where thousands of visitors document their visits.
Take a short drive to this scenic 2-kilometer pathway along the Petitcodiac River with walking trails and river views.
Drive 30 minutes to see the Bay of Fundy's dramatic 40-meter tidal rock formations and explore the ocean floor at low tide.

food.detail: A traditional Maritime dish of shredded potatoes mixed with chicken and gravy baked in a pastry crust, widely available at local restaurants.
Sweet Atlantic lobster meat served on a toasted bun, a quintessential Maritime seafood experience found throughout Moncton.
Moncton's beloved spiced meat sandwich topped with sweet donair sauce, a regional specialty that reflects the city's cultural diversity.
Fresh Bay of Fundy mussels steamed in white wine and garlic, commonly served at seafood restaurants near the attraction.
French fries with gravy and cheese curds, often made with Acadian ingredients or seafood variations at area establishments.

culture.detail: It's customary to let your vehicle roll freely and capture the moment, but always respect the designated area and other visitors trying the illusion.
Summer months bring peak crowds and longer daylight hours for photographing the hill, while winter conditions may affect the road experience.
The Moncton region celebrates Acadian culture with festivals and traditions; respectfully learning about local Acadian history enriches your visit.
Maritime weather can change quickly, so bring layers and plan visits during clear conditions for the best optical illusion effect and photographs.

places.name: Campobello Island
places.description: A picturesque island connected by bridge to Maine, famous as the summer home of President Franklin D. Roosevelt and now a museum dedicated to his legacy. The island offers charming coastal scenery, lighthouses, and a bridge connecting Canada to the United States.

activities.text: Franklin D. Roosevelt Presidential Library & Museum
activities.detail: Tour the restored 34-room summer cottage where FDR and his family vacationed, featuring original furnishings and exhibits on his presidency.
Hike to this iconic red-and-white striped lighthouse perched on a rocky point with stunning Bay of Fundy views, accessible at low tide.
Visit this picturesque white lighthouse on a tidal island that becomes accessible when the Bay of Fundy recedes at low tide.
Explore coastal trails, pebble beaches, and dramatic ocean cliffs while watching for whales and seabirds in the Bay of Fundy.
Walk or drive the historic Roosevelt Campobello International Park bridge connecting Canada to Lubec, Maine, with interpretive signage about its 1962 construction.
Experience the world's highest tides by visiting beaches at different times to observe dramatic water level changes of up to 45 feet.

food.detail: Fresh Atlantic lobster meat piled onto a toasted bun, a Maritime staple available at local coastal restaurants and casual eateries.
Locally harvested edible seaweed from the Bay of Fundy, served dried as a snack or incorporated into seafood dishes.
Battered and deep-fried Atlantic fish such as cod or haddock, typically served with hand-cut fries at waterfront establishments.
Cold-smoked Atlantic salmon prepared locally and sold at island markets, reflecting the region's strong fishing heritage.
Wild blueberries from the Maritime provinces baked into traditional pie, a beloved dessert at local bakeries and restaurants.

culture.detail: The island operates with both Canadian dollars and is close to the US border, so visitors should carry both currencies and bring valid passports or travel documents.
Many island attractions like East Quoddy Lighthouse are only accessible during low tide, so check tide tables before planning outdoor activities.
The island experiences harsh maritime winters and many attractions have limited hours or close from November through April, so plan visits during summer and early fall.
The Bay of Fundy ecosystem is protected and culturally significant to local Mi'kmaq communities, so visitors should stay on marked trails and respect wildlife viewing guidelines.

places.name: River Valley Scenic Drive
places.description: Following the Saint John River through central New Brunswick, this scenic route winds through pastoral landscapes, historic villages, and charming riverside communities. The drive showcases rural New Brunswick's natural beauty and cultural heritage at a leisurely pace.

activities.detail: Paddle the calm waters of the Saint John River to experience the scenic valley from the water, with opportunities to spot bald eagles and enjoy riverside wildlife.
Explore local history and artifacts in Fredericton's premier museum, including exhibits on Acadian and Mi'kmaq heritage.
Visit this park along the river for hiking trails, riverside picnicking, and views of Mactaquac Dam.
Stroll through charming riverside communities like Oromocto and Lincoln to see heritage architecture and local crafts shops.
Explore this 27-acre botanical garden in Saint John featuring themed gardens, walking paths, and seasonal displays.
The Saint John River is renowned for Atlantic salmon fishing, with licensed outfitters available during season.

food.detail: A traditional Acadian dish made from grated potatoes, onions, and meat baked in a savory pie, reflecting the region's French heritage.
Atlantic salmon caught from the Saint John River, typically prepared grilled or smoked at local restaurants and fish camps.
A Maritime twist on Middle Eastern flavors, featuring spiced meat wrapped in pita with sweet sauce, popular in local eateries.
New Brunswick's signature dessert using locally foraged wild blueberries baked in flaky pastry, available at village bakeries.
Local seafood specialty featuring chunks of fresh lobster meat on a toasted roll, reflecting the Maritimes' coastal traditions.

culture.detail: The Saint John River is central to local identity; locals appreciate visitors who engage respectfully with fishing traditions and riverside communities.
This route is meant for unhurried travel; plan extra time to stop in small villages and chat with residents rather than rushing through.
The drive passes through Acadian settlements; understanding the distinct French-speaking culture and history enriches the experience.
Historic villages feature independent craft shops and galleries run by local artists; purchases directly support the community.

places.name: Bathurst
places.description: A charming coastal town on the Acadian Peninsula known for its salmon fishing heritage and welcoming Acadian culture. Bathurst offers excellent seafood, river activities, and serves as a gateway to the scenic northern coast.

activities.text: Salmon Fishing on the Nepisiguit River
activities.detail: The Nepisiguit River is famous for Atlantic salmon and offers guided fishing trips from spring through fall for both experienced and novice anglers.
This museum showcases the region's rich mining heritage with exhibits on underground coal mining operations that once defined the community.
A sandy beach along Chaleur Bay offering swimming, picnicking, and views of the Acadian landscape with supervised swimming during summer months.
A scenic route along the northern coast offering stunning views of Chaleur Bay, traditional Acadian villages, and coastal landscapes.
This riverside park and cultural site celebrates the region's French colonial history and natural beauty with walking trails and interpretive panels.
Local outfitters offer guided kayak trips on the Nepisiguit River and Chaleur Bay to explore the coastline and spot wildlife.

food.detail: Bathurst's signature dish features locally caught salmon prepared simply grilled or smoked to highlight the river's prized catch.
A traditional Acadian dish made with grated potatoes, chicken or seafood, and broth baked in a savory pastry crust.
Creamy chowder loaded with locally sourced lobster, mussels, and white fish reflecting the bay's abundant maritime resources.
A Maritime specialty featuring spiced meat in pita bread with sweet donair sauce, adapted with local seafood variations in Bathurst.
A delicacy harvested from Chaleur Bay, served fresh or in local restaurants as a premium seafood experience.

culture.detail: Bathurst is deeply rooted in Acadian culture; visitors will notice French spoken alongside English, and local festivals celebrate Acadian traditions throughout the year.
The community's identity is built on salmon and seafood heritage, so showing interest in local fishing stories and visiting fishing-related sites is warmly received.
Summer months, particularly July and August, bring Acadian festivals and the peak fishing season, making it the ideal time to experience the town's vibrant cultural calendar.
Bathurst residents are known for their genuine welcoming nature; locals appreciate visitors who engage with the community and show curiosity about Acadian life and traditions.

places.name: Kouchibouguac National Park
places.description: A coastal national park featuring pristine beaches, sand dunes, salt marshes, and abundant wildlife on the Acadian Peninsula. The park offers excellent swimming, kayaking, hiking, and opportunities to observe diverse bird and marine species.

activities.detail: The park's most popular sandy beach offering warm saltwater swimming and ideal conditions for families during summer months.
Paddle through the protected salt marsh ecosystems to observe herons, egrets, and other waterfowl in their natural habitat.
Walk the interpretive trails across active sand dunes to understand coastal geology and spot marram grass and beach vegetation.
Observe over 200 bird species including piping plovers, sandpipers, and migratory waterfowl, particularly during spring and fall migration.
Paddle the gentle river that winds through the park's interior, offering views of forests, wetlands, and opportunities to spot deer and beavers.
Attend ranger-led interpretive talks and slide shows at the campground amphitheater to learn about Acadian ecology and local history.

food.detail: A traditional savory meat pie made with grated potatoes, chicken or pork, and onions, reflecting the Acadian culinary heritage of the region.
Locally caught lobster meat served on a toasted bun, showcasing the Maritime seafood that defines coastal New Brunswick dining.
Mussels harvested from local waters and prepared steamed with white wine and garlic, highlighting the park's marine resources.
A spiced meat sandwich wrapped in pita bread and topped with sweet donair sauce, a beloved Maritime street food with Acadian roots.
Dessert made with wild blueberries foraged from coastal regions, showcasing the sweet berry tradition of Atlantic Canada.

culture.detail: Piping plover nesting areas are periodically closed to protect endangered bird populations, so always check signage and heed closure notices.
The Acadian Peninsula has deep roots in French Maritime culture; understanding the local Acadian heritage enriches your appreciation of the region's identity and traditions.
The Bay of Fundy region experiences significant tidal changes; plan beach activities and kayaking around tide schedules for safety and optimal experience.
Atlantic coastal weather is unpredictable with frequent fog and wind; locals dress in layers and always carry rain gear regardless of forecast.

places.name: Covered Bridges
places.description: New Brunswick boasts the highest number of covered bridges in Canada, with over a dozen historic wooden structures spanning scenic rivers throughout the province. These charming bridges represent important pieces of heritage and offer picturesque photography opportunities.

activities.detail: Stroll across Canada's longest covered bridge at 391 meters, built in 1901, and enjoy riverside views from both ends.

activities.text: Photography Tour at Covered Bridge Scenic Routes
activities.detail: Drive the Acadian Peninsula and Central New Brunswick routes to capture multiple historic bridges in their natural landscape settings.
Join interpretive walks led by local historians who explain the engineering, history, and construction techniques of these wooden structures.

activities.text: Salmon Fishing on Covered Bridge Rivers
activities.detail: Fish the Miramichi and Saint John rivers beneath and near covered bridges during salmon season for an authentic local experience.

activities.text: Picnic and Sketching at Bridge Viewpoints
activities.detail: Pack local provisions and spend time drawing or painting at designated scenic overlooks near structures like the Covered Bridge in Florenceville.
Explore exhibits on bridge construction, engineering innovations, and the cultural significance of these structures in New Brunswick heritage.

food.detail: A savory potato and meat pie that is a signature Acadian dish found throughout New Brunswick's covered bridge regions.
Sample locally caught Atlantic salmon prepared simply grilled or smoked, sourced from rivers flowing beneath the covered bridges.
Try this traditional Maritime seaweed harvested from New Brunswick's Bay of Fundy waters, often eaten as a crispy snack.
Enjoy fresh or baked blueberries from local farms, featuring prominently in pies, jams, and desserts at regional eateries.
Taste fresh Atlantic lobster served on toasted bread, a classic Maritime delicacy available throughout the province.

culture.detail: Visit May through October when bridges are fully accessible, roads are clear, and local festivals celebrate bridge heritage with traditional crafts and tours.
Drive slowly across bridges, stay on designated paths, and avoid heavy vehicles; these wooden structures are fragile and require visitor care to preserve them for future generations.
New Brunswick residents often reference covered bridges by their locations and river names rather than formal titles, so ask locals for directions using geographical landmarks.

places.name: Miramichi
places.description: A legendary salmon fishing destination on the north shore, Miramichi is renowned for its world-class Atlantic salmon river and traditional maritime culture. The town celebrates its heritage through festivals and offers excellent fishing, outdoor activities, and local hospitality.

activities.text: Atlantic Salmon Fishing on the Miramichi River
activities.detail: Cast for legendary Atlantic salmon in one of North America's most productive salmon rivers, best during spring and summer runs.
Learn the history of Atlantic salmon conservation and traditional fishing methods through exhibits and artifacts in Doaktown.
Walk along this historic waterfront area in downtown Miramichi to see traditional fishing boats and the river heritage.
Canoe or kayak through pristine river valleys and forests on the main branch of the Miramichi for wildlife viewing and scenic beauty.
Drive the scenic route along the Acadian Peninsula to nearby villages, coastal views, and traditional settlements.
Experience the town's Irish heritage through music, dancing, and traditional food at this annual celebration drawing visitors worldwide.

food.detail: Locally smoked salmon prepared using traditional methods, a quintessential Miramichi delicacy reflecting the river's bounty.
Fresh Atlantic lobster meat on soft bread, readily available at local restaurants and reflecting the Maritime seafood tradition.
Battered and deep-fried local fish served with hand-cut fries, a staple at waterfront establishments and casual eateries.
A traditional potato and meat dish from the nearby Acadian Peninsula, found at local restaurants celebrating regional heritage.
Harvested seaweed snacks and ingredients used in local cooking, representing the Atlantic coastal food culture.

culture.detail: Atlantic salmon fishing is strictly regulated with specific seasons and licensing requirements; always check current rules before fishing.
The region is traditional Mi'kmaq territory; visitors should acknowledge Indigenous connections to the river and its resources.
Miramichi culture revolves around the tides and salmon runs; pace your visit around natural rhythms rather than rushing activities.
The town celebrates strong Irish and Scottish settler heritage through festivals, music sessions, and local storytelling traditions.

restaurants.name: Acadian Coast
restaurants.description: Authentic Acadian cuisine celebrating Maritime traditions with locally-sourced ingredients and regional specialties.

restaurants.name: The Rosewood Pub
restaurants.description: Historic uptown gastropub serving elevated comfort food with craft cocktails in a warmly-lit brick setting.

restaurants.name: Catch Restaurant
restaurants.description: Contemporary seafood restaurant overlooking the Bay of Fundy with daily catches and refined coastal preparations.

restaurants.name: Winsome Brewing Company
restaurants.description: Craft brewery and restaurant featuring locally-brewed beers paired with creative small plates and charcuterie.

restaurants.name: L'Arôme
restaurants.description: Refined French bistro offering classic Gallic cuisine in an intimate, elegant setting with attentive service.

restaurants.name: Tiramisu Bistro
restaurants.description: Casual Italian bistro known for authentic pastas, risottos, and warm hospitality in a charming neighbourhood spot.

restaurants.name: Tide & Boar
restaurants.description: Farm-to-table restaurant celebrating local New Brunswick produce and proteins with seasonal, inventive menus.
restaurants.mustOrder: Pan-Seared Local Fish with Root Vegetables

restaurants.name: Sabai Thai
restaurants.description: Vibrant Thai restaurant delivering authentic Southeast Asian flavours with curries, noodles, and fresh preparations.

restaurants.name: Piazza Pizzeria
restaurants.description: Casual pizzeria and Italian eatery serving wood-fired pizzas and traditional pastas in a friendly, laid-back atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Rosario's Ristorante
restaurants.description: Upscale Italian restaurant with chef-driven cuisine, extensive wine list, and romantic ambiance for special occasions.

restaurants.name: The Dippery
restaurants.description: Relaxed waterfront spot serving classic fish and chips, lobster rolls, and Maritime fare with bay views.

restaurants.name: Francophone Kitchen
restaurants.description: Modern take on Acadian heritage cuisine, blending traditional recipes with contemporary techniques and presentation.

restaurants.name: Split Crow Pub
restaurants.description: Historic Irish pub with traditional décor, live music, hearty pub fare, and craft beers in a lively social setting.

restaurants.name: Chef's Restaurant
restaurants.description: Fine dining establishment offering creative cuisine that blends global influences with local Maritime ingredients.
